questions.question: At almost 8 weeks old, Adelina flashed her very first smile. Who was the lucky audience?
questions.follow_up_context: At just under 8 weeks old, Adelina smiled at both Mommy and Daddy — a social smile, which is different from the reflex smiles newborns do in their sleep. A social smile means she recognized faces she loves and responded to them. Big deal!

questions.question: At 3 months old, Adelina gave her very first frown to who?
questions.follow_up_context: Tony was the recipient of Adelina's debut frown at 3 months old. She was already forming opinions.

questions.question: At 3 months, Adelina's babbling suddenly went from zero to...
questions.answer: B) Exponentially more — she couldn't stop
options: A) Quieter — she got more serious
B) Exponentially more — she couldn't stop
questions.follow_up_context: Around 3 months, Adelina's babbling exploded. Babies this age are discovering their voice and experimenting with sounds — it's the very beginning of language development.

questions.question: At 3.5 months, Adelina made her first big physical move — literally. What did she do?
questions.answer: B) Rolled to her side on her own
options: A) Rolled all the way over
B) Rolled to her side on her own
C) Pushed up on her arms
D) Sat up with no support
questions.follow_up_context: Not a full roll yet — but rolling to her side at 3.5 months is a real milestone. She was building the core strength and coordination that leads to full rolling.

questions.question: At 3.5 months, Adelina hit a big nighttime milestone. What was it?
options: B) Moved to her own room
questions.follow_up_context: Dropping nighttime feedings at 3.5 months is a huge deal — for Adelina and for the adults in the house. Her little body had grown enough to make it through the night without refueling.

questions.question: On Valentine's Day, at exactly 4 months old, Adelina discovered a bold new use for her voice. What was it?
questions.follow_up_context: Nothing says Happy Valentine's Day like a baby discovering she can shriek. At 4 months, babies often find their upper vocal range and are delighted by the results. The adults are... also experiencing something.

questions.question: At 4 months, Adelina graduated to her crib. How was she already sleeping when she made the move?
questions.follow_up_context: Already arms out at 4 months! Babies who start breaking out of swaddles are usually ready to transition — and Adelina was ahead of the curve.

questions.question: At 4.5 months, Adelina figured out rolling — which direction did she master first?
options: C) Both at the same time
D) She skipped rolling and went straight to crawling
questions.follow_up_context: Front to back usually comes first — it's a little easier because gravity helps. Adelina nailed it at 4.5 months, right on track.

questions.question: Just four days after her first roll, what did Adelina do?
questions.answer: B) Rolled both ways — multiple times
options: A) Refused to roll at all
B) Rolled both ways — multiple times
C) Rolled so much she fell off the mat
questions.follow_up_context: Four days after mastering front-to-back, Adelina was rolling front to back AND back to front, multiple times in a row. Once babies get rolling, they go all in.

questions.question: At nearly 5 months, Adelina hit two milestones in one day. What were they?
questions.answer: B) First time sleeping on her tummy + started sleep training
options: B) First time sleeping on her tummy + started sleep training
C) First solid food + first clap
D) First word + first high five
questions.follow_up_context: A big day: Adelina slept on her stomach for the first time, and the family started fuss-it-out sleep training. Once babies can roll both ways, tummy sleeping is considered safe — because they can reposition themselves.

questions.question: At 5 months old, Adelina made an exciting discovery about her own body. What did she find?
questions.answer: C) Her toes — and she could grab them
options: A) Her reflection in the mirror
C) Her toes — and she could grab them
questions.follow_up_context: Grabbing toes at 5 months is a classic baby milestone — it means her core strength and flexibility have developed enough to fold herself in half. Also, toes are fascinating. Frankly, we get it.
